debug_info.goal:
case pos
x : IGame
p : Player
y : IGame
hy : y ∈ moves p x
z : IGame
hz : z ∈ @IGame.reverseSet x y p
h : @IGame.Reversible x y p
⊢ ¬@IGame.Reversible (@IGame.unreverse x) (@IGame.unreverse z) p

failed_attempts:
The following theorem is false. A counterexample is given below.

The issue is that `unreverse` checks `Reversible x y p` relative to the original game `x`,
but after bypassing a reversible move `y` with the moves of the reversibility witness,
the replacement moves may themselves become reversible relative to the unreversed game
`unreverse x`, even though they were not reversible in the original `x`.

Concretely, if `x = {y | }`, `y = { | w}`, `w = {z | }`, `z = { | 0}`, and `w ≤ x`,
then `y` is reversible in `x` through `w`. The bypass replaces `y` with `z` (a left option
of `w`). After unreversing, `unreverse x = {unreverse z | } = {{ | 0} | }`. But `z = { | 0}`
has right option `0 ≤ unreverse x`, making `unreverse z` reversible in `unreverse x`.

/-- `unreverse x` has no reversible positions. -/
theorem not_reversible_unreverse (hy : y ∈ x.unreverse.moves p) : ¬ Reversible x.unreverse y p := by
  rw [moves_unreverse, Set.mem_iUnion₂] at hy
  obtain ⟨y, hy, ⟨z, hz, rfl⟩⟩ := hy
  by_cases h : Reversible x y p
  · sorry
  · rw [reverseSet_of_not_reversible h] at hz
    subst hz
    sorry
-/

/-! #### Counterexample to `not_reversible_unreverse` -/

private def cex_z : IGame := !{∅ | {(0 : IGame)}}
private def cex_w : IGame := !{{cex_z} | ∅}
private def cex_y : IGame := !{∅ | {cex_w}}
private def cex_x : IGame := !{{cex_y} | ∅}

private theorem cex_zero_le_w : (0 : IGame) ≤ cex_w := by
  rw [le_iff_forall_lf]; simp [cex_w]

private theorem cex_zero_le_x : (0 : IGame) ≤ cex_x := by
  rw [le_iff_forall_lf]; simp [cex_x]

private theorem cex_w_le_x : cex_w ≤ cex_x := by
  rw [le_iff_forall_lf]; simp [cex_w, cex_x, cex_y, cex_z]
  intro h; exact (le_iff_forall_lf.mp h).2 0 (by simp [cex_z]) cex_zero_le_x

/-- There exist games where `unreverse x` still has reversible positions.
This disproves the would-be theorem `not_reversible_unreverse`. -/
theorem exists_reversible_unreverse :
    ∃ (x : IGame) (p : Player) (y : IGame),
      y ∈ x.unreverse.moves p ∧ Reversible x.unreverse y p := by
  refine ⟨cex_x, Player.left, unreverse cex_z, ?_, ?_⟩
  · have rev : Reversible cex_x cex_y Player.left :=
      ⟨cex_w, by simp [cex_y], cex_w_le_x⟩
    have hmyY : cex_y ∈ cex_x.moves Player.left := by simp [cex_x]
    have hchoose : rev.choose = cex_w := by
      have := rev.choose_mem_moves; simp [cex_y] at this; exact this
    exact mem_moves_unreverse_of_reversible rev hmyY (by rw [hchoose]; simp [cex_w])
  · rw [reversible_left]
    refine ⟨unreverse 0, ?_, ?_⟩
    · exact mem_moves_unreverse_of_not_reversible
        (by simp [cex_z] : (0 : IGame) ∈ cex_z.moves Player.right)
        (by rw [reversible_right]; simp)
    · calc unreverse 0 ≤ (0 : IGame) := (unreverse_equiv 0).1
        _ ≤ cex_x := cex_zero_le_x
        _ ≤ unreverse cex_x := (unreverse_equiv cex_x).2
